source.title: DGMS Guidance — Gas Testing, Continuous Monitoring & Confined Working
source.authority: Directorate General of Mines Safety (DGMS), Ministry of Labour & Employment, Government of India
source.revision: Mines Act 1952 framework; Coal Mines Regulations (CMR) 2017; DGMS (Tech.) Circulars
source.provenance: DGMS regulates safety in mines (not factories). Its gas-testing, competent-person and continuous-monitoring provisions are cited here by analogy because the Battery-4 inspection chamber is a confined, gas-liable working of exactly the kind DGMS provisions were written for, and the problem statement lists DGMS as a compliance dimension. Clause texts are faithful summaries; verify against the source circular/regulation before operational use.
source.note: Included to give the compound engine a monitoring-and-competency lens alongside the Factories Act (statutory) and OISD (process good-practice) sources.

clauses.title: Gas testing before and during work by a certified competent person
clauses.summary: Testing for inflammable and noxious gases in gas-liable workings shall be carried out before work begins and repeated during the work by a certified competent gas-testing person using approved detectors. A single check at one location and one time is inadequate where the atmosphere can change as work proceeds or as connected pressures shift.

clauses.title: Continuous CH4/CO monitoring with automatic withdrawal thresholds
clauses.summary: In gassy workings, continuous tele-monitoring of methane (CH4) and carbon monoxide (CO) shall be provided, with automatic cut-off of power and withdrawal of persons when CH4 reaches about 0.75% or noxious gas such as CO reaches about 50 ppm. The threshold response is a last-line trip: it reacts at the alarm limit, not to the trend building toward it.

clauses.title: Proactive prevention of inflammable-gas hazards
clauses.summary: Where gas content is significant, inflammable-gas hazards shall be managed proactively — through advance monitoring and control — so that gas does not reach an ignition-capable accumulation, rather than relying on reaction once a dangerous concentration has already formed.
